coredata.dc:title: Preparation of Carbon Dots and Their Application in Food Analysis as Signal Probe

coredata.dc:creator: YE, Song-Ling
HUANG, Jia-Jia
LUO, Lin
FU, Hui-Jun
SUN, Yuan-Ming
SHEN, Yu-Dong
LEI, Hong-Tao
XU, Zhen-Lin
coredata.dc:description: Abstract As a new class of fluorescent nanoparticles, carbon dots have potential to replace semiconductor quantum dots due to their excellent optical properties, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, facile synthesis, low cost and easy functionalization. By virtue of characteristics mentioned above, carbon dots-based fluorescent probes showed great potential in the field of food analysis. In this paper, we reviewed the production of carbon dots, their properties and applications in food analysis such as food additives detection, metal ion monitoring, food borne pathogens detection, organic pollutants and food nutritional components analysis. The challenges and perspectives of carbon dots in this exciting and promising field were also discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Reparation
Food analysis
Application
Review

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: CHINESE JOURNAL OF ANALYTICAL CHEMISTRY Volume 45, Issue 10, October 2017 Online English edition of the Chinese language journal Cite this article as: Chin J Anal Chem, 2017, 45(10), 1571â€“1581. ________________________ Received 8 March 2017; accepted 4 August 2017 *Corresponding author. Email: jallent@163.com This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2016YFD0401204, 2016YFE0106000), the Guangdong Natural Science Funds for Distinguished Young Scholar of China (No. 2014A030306026), the Guangdong Special Support Program of China (No. 2014TQ01N109), and the Guangdong Provincial Science and Technology Projects of China (No. 2016A040403077). Copyright Â© 2017, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier Limited. All rights reserved. DOI: 10.1016/S1872-2040(17)61045-4 REVIEW Preparation of Carbon Dots and Their Application in Food Analysis as Signal Probe YE Song-Ling1, HUANG Jia-Jia1,2, LUO Lin1, FU Hui-Jun1, SUN Yuan-Ming1, SHEN Yu-Dong1, LEI Hong-Tao1, XU Zhen-Lin1,* 1 Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Safety and Quality, College of Food Science, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510640, China 2 Guangdong Food and Drug Vocational College, Guangzhou 510520, China Abstract: As a new class of fluorescent nanoparticles, carbon dots have potential to replace semiconductor quantum dots due to their excellent optical properties, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, facile synthesis, low cost and easy functionalization. By virtue of characteristics mentioned above, carbon dots-based fluorescent probes showed great potential in the field of food analysis. In this paper, we reviewed the production of carbon dots, their properties and applications in food analysis such as food additives detection, metal ion monitoring, food borne pathogens detection, organic pollutants and food nutritional components analysis. The challenges and perspectives of carbon dots in this exciting and promising field were also discussed. Key Words: Carbon dots; Reparation; Food analysis; Application; Review 1 Introduction Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of zero-dimensional fluorescent quasispherical nanoparticles with sizes below 10 nm. It was first discovered in 2004 by Xuâ€™s group[1]. They found that a novel class of viable fluorescent nanomaterials could fluoresce different colors at 365 nm of UV light during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWNTS) derived from arc-discharge soot. The name of carbon dots was defined by Sun et al[2]. in the study of obtaining carbon nanoparticles with good fluorescence properties through laser etching in 2006. CDs have the same excellent optical properties as traditional quantum dots (QDs), such as good photostability, wide excitation and emission spectra, and large stokes shifts. However, in comparison with QDs, CDs possess lower or no toxicity and better biocompatibility without toxic heavy metal element. Moreover, with the abundant hydrophilic groups such as carboxyl, amine or hydroxyl groups on the surface, CDs is imparted excellent water solubility and easy modification. For these reasons, CDs have attracted significant attention and are widely applied in areas such as bioimaging[3â€“5], biochemical analysis[6â€“8] and photoelectrocatalysis in the past decades[9,10]. Although CDs-based probe harbors great potential in food analysis, the applications of CDs in the field of food analysis are not enough. In this review, we highlighted the latest development on the synthesis strategies, optical and electronic properties of CDs as well as their applications in food analysis, which includes analysis of food nutritional components, food additives and heavy metal ions, and the detection about food borne pathogens and harmful organic pollutants. Additionally, current challenges and future prospects of CDs for fundamental studies and applications are also discussed. We hope this article would offer valuable insight for the investigation of CDs and further expand the boundaries of their applications. YE Song-Ling et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2017, 45(10): 1571â€“1581 2 Structures and properties of CDs CDs are usually a nanometer scale sphere or quasi sphere processing three-dimensional cluster structure[11]. Different particle size of CDs can be obtained by controlling reaction temperature[12], reaction time[13] and other factors in the synthesis of CDs. However, the formation mechanism has not been clearly explained and need to further study[14]. Generally, there are many water-soluble groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl on the CDs surface, which endue CDs excellent water solubility and amenability for surface modification. In comparison with the traditional semiconductor QDs, CDs have the following outstanding characteristics. (1) Currently, most of CDs display a strong light absorption in the ultraviolet region, usually at 270â€“320 nm. The wavelength may increase to visible region after surface passivation. (2) Similar to traditional QDs, CDs have a broad and continuous excitation spectrum, different emission wavelengths can be obtained under excitation of a single wavelength[2], which provides the possibilities of simultaneous detection of multi-component of biomolecules[15,16]. (3) With high photoluminescence intensity and resistance to photo-bleaching, fluorescence intensity of CDs is hardly reduced in continuously excitations[17]. (4) The fluorescence intensity of CDs is adjustable. A significant effect on the fluorescence intensity could be obtained with adjusting pH value of the solution or the synthetic routes in the preparation of the QDs [18â€“20]. (5) Because of the photoelectric transfer characteristics, CDs can be used as excellent electron donors and electron acceptors[21,22]. (6) Some CDs possess the property of up-converted photoluminescence (UPCL), which emits a shorter wavelength fluorescence under the excitation of a longer wavelength[23,24]. Shen et al[25] reported that with the passivation by PEG, when the excitation wavelength was 600â€“800 nm, the up-conversion wavelength of CDs shifted to 390â€“468 nm. (7) Apart from the photoluminescence properties, CDs also exhibit electrochemical luminescence (ECL) properties similar to the semiconductor QDs[26]. According to the previous works, the electroluminescence properties of CDs are not affected by particle size or the modifier, but incline to be surface state-dependent. The ECL and photoluminescence (PL) mechanisms of CDs are shown in Fig.1. Moreover, unlike traditional metal-containing QDs, CDs mainly contain four elements, C, O, H and N, implying its low toxicity and good biocompatibility[27,28]. Additionally, some CDs have two- photon absorption[29], high salt resistance[30], quasi-enzyme catalysis[31], near-infrared emission[32] and other excellent characteristics, which means that CDs hold great application potential in many areas. 3 Preparation of CDs To establish convenient and economical preparation method, and to acquire CDs with better luminescence properties, a series of new methods for the synthesis of CDs have been built through unremitting efforts of researchers. And these methods can be divided into two general strategies: top-down and bottom-up methods. Top-down methods are mainly realized by chemical, electrochemical or physical approaches to break down carbon precursors to nano-scale ones, such as arc discharge[1,34], laser ablation[2,35,36], electrochemical oxidation[17,37,38] and chemical oxidation[39â€’41] and so on. On the contrary, bottom-up methods usually use small molecules as the reaction precursors, mainly via chemical synthesis, including template method[42â€“44], microwave[45â€“47] and hydrothermal synthesis[48â€“50]. The crude products obtained by the above methods need to be further purified by the centrifugation, dialysis or electrophoresis. In general, by top-down methods, the CDs could obtain a better performance, while some drawbacks including complicated procedures, difficult collection, expensive instruments and lower fluorescence quantum yield did exist. In contrast, for bottom-up methods, synthetic conditions are easier to be controlled. Carbon sources is abundant, and the synthesized CDs usually possess higher fluorescence quantum yield, but the following-up purification steps are more difficult, thus posing great impact on the performance of CDs. Currently, bottom-up method is the predominant strategy for CDs synthesis. In recent years, microwave and hydrothermal methods are favored by scientists for their resource availability and environment friendliness. Typical preparation methods and the performance parameters of the obtained CDs are summarized in Table 1 and Table 2. 4 Application of CDs in food analysis and detection With conventional semiconductor QDs, recombination efficiency between the electron hole pairs on the surface can be changed by the interaction between CDs and the analyte. And it results in the enhancement or quenching of the fluorescence, thus achieving qualitative or quantitative analysis of the analyte[51]. CDs-based fluorescent probe has some advantages such as simplicity, high sensitivity and selectivity, and its application in food analysis and detection has attracted more and more attention and research interests. Fig.1 Electrochemical luminescence and photoluminescence of carbon dots[26] YE Song-Ling et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2017, 45(10): 1571â€“1581 Table 1 Preparation of carbon dots by top-down methods and its performance parameters Method Carbon source Modification or doping Size (nm) Quantum yield (%) Excitation wavelength (nm) Emission wavelength (nm) Ref. Arc discharge Arc-discharge soot â€”a 0.96 1.6 366 â€” [1] Arc discharge SWNTsb â€” â€” â€” 276â€“375 420â€“544 [34] Laser ablation Graphite powder, cement PEG1500N 3â€“10 4â€“10 400 â€” [2] Laser ablation Carbon powder PEG200N 1â€“8 3â€“8 420 490 [35] Laser ablation Commercial nano-carbon material â€” < 50 â€” 380 450 [36] Electrochemical oxidation MWNTsc â€” 2.8 Â± 0.5 6.4 365 410 [37] Electrochemical oxidation Graphite â€” 1.9 Â± 0.3 3.2 Â± 0.5 1.2 330 370 445 510 [17] Electrochemical oxidation Graphite rod â€” 1.2â€“3.8 12 240â€“580 350â€“800 [38] Chemical oxidation Carbon soot â€” 2â€“6 3 450 520 [39] Chemical oxidation Acetic acid P2O5 < 5 â€” 375â€“430 450â€“500 [40] Chemical oxidation Activated carbon â€” 5â€“10 70 365 540 [41] a: Not mentioned in the original text; b: Single-walled carbon nanotubes; c: Multiwalled carbon nanotubes. Table 2 Preparation of carbon dots by bottom-up methods and Its performance parameters Method Carbon source Modification or doping Size (nm) Quantum yield (%) Excitation wavelength (nm) Emission wavelength (nm) Ref. Template method 2,4-diaminophenol dihydrochloride â€”a 4â€“6 0.1 340 â€” [42] Template method Phenol/Formaldedyderesins PEG1500N 1.5â€“2.5 14.7 360 430â€“580 [43] Template method Citric acid â€” 1.5â€“2.5 23 320â€“520 399â€“559 [44] Microwave Glycerol TTDDAb 3.5 12.02 340â€“480 450â€“550 [45] Microwave Phytic acid Ethylenediamine 9 19.5 Â± 1.5 400 525 [46] Microwave Chitosan â€” 7.0 â€” 300â€“440 420â€“500 [47] Hydrothermal synthesis Orange juice â€” 1.5â€“4.5 26 405â€“488 â€” [48] Hydrothermal synthesis Peach blossom â€” 4.4 Â± 0.8 26 370 441 [49] Hydrothermal synthesis Carbon soot â€” 2â€“3 25.44 320 420 [50] a: Not mentioned in the original text; b: 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine. 4.1 Detection of food additives Food additives are widely used in food processing for improving its flavor, shelf life and nutritional value. Meanwhile, the abuse of food additives time and again has become one of potential factors causing food safety problem. Therefore, it is essential to develop a rapid and reliable detection method for food additives to strengthen the monitoring of food additives. Lin et al[52] synthesized serine-modified CDs by microwave-assisted approach with glycerine as carbon source and PEG 1500 as passivator. In the presence of nitrite and acidified hydrogen peroxide, the CDs exhibited chemiluminescent (CL) property, thus a sensitive method for quantitative detection of nitrite was developed. The CL intensity increased linearly with nitrite concentration in the range from 1.0 Ã—10â€“7 M to 1.0 Ã— 10â€“5 M. On the basis of this, the method was successfully applied to detect nitrites in pure milk. Meanwhile, Yuan et al[53] synthesized CDs by using non-fluorescent chelating ligand N-(2-hydroxyethyl) ethylenediaminetriacetic acid (HEDTA) as carbon source. These CDs could emit strong blue fluorescence, and sunset yellow, a food colorant, could selectively quench the fluorescence through fluorescent resonance energy transfer (FRET), therefore a CDs-based nanosensor for the determination of sunset yellow was developed. The working range was from 0.3 ÂµM to 8.0 ÂµM, with a limit of detection of 79.6 nM. Xu et al[54] utilized fresh aloe as precusor to synthesize CDs and it was found that, tartrazine could efficiently quench the fluorescence of the CDs through a static quenching process (Fig.2). Taking advantages of this quenching phenomenon, a fluorescent probe for detection of tartrazine was developed with a linear range of 0.25â€“32.5 ÂµM and a detection limit of 73 nM. This probe was also employed to detect trace level of tartrazine in some food samples including steamed buns, honey and candy. Due to the simplicity YE Song-Ling et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2017, 45(10): 1571â€“1581 Fig.2 Preparation of carbon dots with aloe vera as a carbon source for detection of tartrazine[54] and high sensitivity, fluorescence spectroscopy has been gradually applied to detect food additives in recent years[55â€“58]. The advantages of CDs used in this regard would be highlighted by ameliorating the sample pretreatment and thus further improving the sensitivity and selectivity. 4.2 Detection of metal ions Some common metal ions, such as aluminum (Al3+), mercury (Hg2+) and copper (Cu2+) ions, are not biodegradable and their accumulation in human bodies through food chain can cause various health problems. Therefore, metal ion detection is an essential part in food safety monitoring, especially for toxic heavy metal ions detection[59,60]. Fluorescence quenching resulted from interaction between CDs and metal ions, is always used to fabricate turn-off sensor for rapid and sensitive detection of metal ions. Yan et al[61] utilized citric acid anhydrous as carbon source and 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine as passivation ligand to synthesize CDs by a one-step hydrothermal method. In addition, such CDs were served as an effective fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective detection of Al3+ (Fig.3). The relative fluorescence intensity of this sensing platform exhibits a linear response to Al3+ concentration ranging from 4 ÂµM to 40 ÂµM, with a detection limit of 0.0329 ÂµM. Nitrogen doped carbon nanoparticles (N-doped CNPs) as a novel fluorescent probe targeting on Hg2+with a limit of detection of 2.3 nM was reported by Liu et al[62]. According to Liuâ€™s work, N-doped CNPs were prepared through a facile one-pot and solid-phase synthesis approach. This sensing mechanism was on the basis of the stronger interaction between pyrrolic-like N or Nâ€’H on the surface of the N-dropped CNPs and Hg2+ than other metal ions. This assay was employed for quick detection of Hg2+ in milk and kelp samples with satisfactory results. Specific ions can be specifically detected by surface modified or composite type of CDs in a complex environment. Zhu et al[63] synthesized polyethylenimine (PEI) modified CDs through hydrothermal carbonization of kelp juice, and used them in the dual-signal FL detection of Cu2+ in real yogurt samples. It should be noted that most of CDs-based probe for metal ions sensing are based on the fluorescence quench of CDs resulting from inner filter effect[64,65]. In comparison with traditional detection methods, CDs-based nanosensors have obvious advantages in sensitivity and selectivity. However, current reported CDs-based fluorescent probes mainly focus on the detection of several kinds of metal ions, and minority of them are applied in real food sample analysis due to the complex sample processing. Recently, it was found that simultaneous detection of a variety of metal ions could be achieved by fluorescent sensors constructed with multiple CDs. Dual- Fig.3 Synthesis of carbon dots and its application as a fluorescent probe for Al3+ [61] YE Song-Ling et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2017, 45(10): 1571â€“1581 emitting carbon dot/carbon dot (CD1/CD2) conjugates were prepared by combination of poly (ethylenimine)-functionalized CD1 with carboxyl-modified CD2. And this CD1/CD2 conjugates could be reversibly used for simultaneous sensing of Cu2+ and Hg2+, showing two-photon fluorescence (TPF) emissions peaked at 465 nm (CD1) and 600 nm (CD2). When adding different concentrations of Cu2+ and Hg2+, linear change in the intensity of fluorescence emission at 465 and 600 nm were observed with a limit of detection of 0.05 and 0.08 M, respectively[66]. 4.3 Analysis of food nutritional components By virtue of fluorescence quenching phenomenon resulting from surface electron transfer between CDs and metal ions, some CDs-based turn-on fluorescent sensors were developed for the detection of nutrient content in food with metal chelating ability. The electrostatic interaction between metal ions and CDs on the surface and the electron transfer induced the fluorescence quenching of CDs. Upon adding analyte, the fluorescence of the CDs restored gradually due to the strong chelating effect between the analyte and metal ion, and ultimately achieving the quantitative analysis of the analyte. Glutathione (GSH), as a redox buffer to maintain cellular redox balance in living organism, is widely present in animals, plants and microorganisms[67]. Xu et al[68] developed a fluorescence probe via a â€œswitch-onâ€� strategy for GSH sensing in foods. According to Xuâ€™s work, CDs was prepared via hydrothermal method by mixing sodium citrate with urea, and the fluorescence of CDs was quenched by Hg2+ through electron transfer. In the presence of GSH, the fluorescence sensor was switched to the â€œonâ€� state, which was attributed to the competitive binding to Hg2+ between GSH and the functional groups of CDs. This fluorescence sensor was successfully employed for the determination of GSH in various kinds of food samples with excellent recoveries and limit of detection of 37 nM. In addition, it was reported that Hg2+ could be replaced by lower toxic Cu2+ to achieve efficient detection of GSH in food[69]. Purbia et al[70] prepared highly luminescent CDs by microwave-assisted hydrothermal method (Fig.4). Base on the CDs, a simple â€œturn-onâ€� fluorescent sensor for rapid detection of thiamine (vitamin B1) was developed. The principle of this fluorescent sensor was based on the fact that fluorescence of the CDs could be quenched by Cu2+, and recovered again after the addition of thiamine due to the formation of soluble Cu-thiamine complex. The limit of detection for thiamine was 280 nM with a working range of 10â€“50 ÂµM. The matrix effect of food samples intensity can influence the accuracy of the test results because of the complex food composition. Therefore, it is necessary to develop appropriate pretreatment and synthesize CDs materials with better stability and selectivity for detection. Fig.4 Schematic diagram of fluorescence response of CDs in the presence of copper ions and thiamine[70] YE Song-Ling et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2017, 45(10): 1571â€“1581 4.4 Detection of foodborne pathogens Food safety accidents resulting from foodborne diseases occurred time and again around the world in recent years. As the main cause leading to foodborne diseases, regular foodborne pathogens include Salmonella spp., Escherichia coli, Vibrio parahaemolyticus and so on[71,72]. Xia et al[73] developed a CDs-based fluorescent immunoassay for Salmonella paratyphi B. The antibody against Salmonella O4 factor was conjugated to CDs to be immuno-fluorescent CDs, which were further used to enrich Salmonella paratyphi B in samples, forming a sandwich immuno-complex as anti-O4-CDs-Salmonella paratyphi B-anti-Ha-CDs. After purified with a magnetic separator, the immuno-complex were further cleaved using the immuno-affinity separation buffer to release CDs for the fluorescence detection and the quantitation of Salmonella paratyphi B in samples. This method exhibited a detection limit of 103 CFU/mL. The whole detection took about two hours. Weng et al[74] used a simple one-step dry heating method to synthesize mannose-modified fluorescent CDs (Man-CDs) from solid ammonium citrate and mannose, and successfully applied it in labeling Escherichia coli (Fig.5). The selective binding of mannose to the lectin units of E. coli enabled Man-CDs specifically bind to E. coli, which achieved quantitative detection of E. coli at concentrations as low as 103 CFU/mL in real samples (tap water, apple juice, human urine). It should be noted that appropriate ligands to pathogens is one of the key components to construct CDs-based probe for the detection of foodborne pathogens. Effective ligands should be able to firmly conjugate with the CDs, and meanwhile ensure the stability of chemical and optical properties of the CDs when integrating with the target. Amikacin is a well-known aminoglycoside antibiotic which can be used as a binding ligand towards E. coli. Chandra et al[75] reported a one-step strategy to synthesize amikacin modified fluorescent carbon dots (CDs-amikacin) for assay of E. coli. This method showed a linear range of 3.904 Ã— 105 â€“7.625 Ã— 102 CFU/mL as well as a detection limit of 522 CFU/mL. Furthermore, it was successfully applied in sensing E. coli in various fruit juice samples (apple, pineapple and orange). 4.5 Detection of harmful organic pollutants The hazardous organic pollution in food caused by the widespread use of pesticides and veterinary drugs, has posed a serious threat to food safety. A series of detection methods based on CDs have been established in recent years, including fluorescence assay, immunoassay, and electrochemical sensor. Recently, Chang et al[76] adopted CDs as sensing receptors for the detection of paraoxon-ethyl pesticide on real vegetable samples. The as-synthesized CDs emitted strong yellow fluorescence with the maximum emission at 524 nm when excited at 423 nm. Upon adding paraoxon-ethyl, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched accordingly in a paraoxon-ethyl concentration dependent manner. The probe exhibited a limit of detection of (0.22 Â± 0.02) ÂµM and a dynamic linear range up to 5.80 mM. Fluorescence quenching can also be combined with molecular imprinting and other methods to develop sensors with higher sensitivity and selectivity to further expand their applications. Li et al[77] fabricated a biosensor targeting on lincomycin with a dual recognition system comprising a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) and aptamers selective for lincomycin. The MIP was synthesized by electropolymerization of CDs-tagged DNA aptamers, lincomycin and o-aminophenol on the gold nanoparticle-functionalized graphene oxide (Au-GO)- modified electrode. Electrogenerated chemiluminescence (ECL) resonance energy transfer was observed between Au-GO and CDs. Upon the CDs received the energy, they served Fig.5 Schematic illustration of synthesis of mannose-modified carbon dots (Man-CDs) and their use in selective labeling of E. coli[74] YE Song-Ling et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2017, 45(10): 1571â€“1581 as a signal indicator and displayed enhanced signal intensity in the presence of lincomycin. When lincomycin was competitively bound to DNA aptamers and MIP, it blocked the transfer of energy, and a decreased ECL signal was observed. Thus, a dual recognition method for the detection of lincomycin was realized. Based on this strategy, the sensor exhibited a linear ECL response to lincomycin at concentrations from 5.0 Ã— 10â€“12 M to 1.0 Ã— 10â€“9 M with detection limit of 1.6 Ã— 10â€“13 M. The method was utilized to determine lincomycin residuals in different meat samples with satisfactory results. However, the stability of the DNA aptamer and the imprinted capacity in the sensor needed further improvement. These methods mentioned above can be used for rapid and high sensitivity detection of harmful organic contaminants in food, and some typical methods are listed in Table 3. 5 Conclusions and perspectives In summary, as a novel class of luminescent nanomaterials, CDs have attracted considerable interest in the field of food safety by virtue of their high bright luminescence, low toxicity and good biocompatibility. There is no doubt that CDs-based nanosensor exhibit promising prospect in food safety monitoring. However, the research on CDs is still at its early stage, and their fundamental photoluminescence mechanism remains elusive and should be further researched and elucidated. As for synthesis and preparation of CDs, the precursor is abundant and the synthesis strategies remain diverse and flexible, but it is still in the laboratory stage. Additionally, subsequent purification is often complicated and time consuming, which poses a significant negative effect on the quantum yield and luminescent properties of CDs. For food analysis applications, CDs hold great applications potential in this field, and some issues are still required to be settled. For instance, most of developed CDs-based nanosensors are performed in laboratory environment, and their specificity, sensitivity and stability are needed to be further improved for fitting the complicated food matrix. Meanwhile, most of them only can be used to detect one target in one test and therefore development of CDs-based nanosensors for simultaneous detection of multi analytes will be favored by related researchers. Therefore, it will make a great contribution to the industrialization of CDs by selecting appropriate carbon source and optimizing the synthesis process to improve quantum yield and chemical stability and reproducibility. Moreover, the combination of other methods such as immunoassay, electrochemical sensor, molecularly imprinted polymer, etc., doped or surface functionalized CDs will further broaden the scope of application, improve the sensitivity, accuracy and specificity of the methods. With continual development and application of the immunoassay method and sensor technology in the field of food safety, the combination of CDs with them is expected to provide a promising platform for the supervision and management of food safety. Table 3 Cabon dots-based fluorescent probes for detecting harmful organic pollutants in food Carbon system Detection method Detector Linear range(ÂµM) Limit of detection (ÂµM) Sample Ref. CDs Fluorescence determination Paraoxon 0â€“5.8 Ã— 10â€“6 0.22 Â± 0.02 Bok choy [76] CDs-AgNPs Fluorescence determination Glyphosate 0.15â€“15.0 7 Ã— 10-5 Cereal [78] IgG-CDs Fluorescence immunoassay Glyphosate 0.06â€“473 4.7 Ã— 10â€“5 â€”a [79] N,S-CDs Fluorescence determination Carbaryl â€” 0.025 â€” [80] CDs-Cu(II) Fluorescence determination Dichlorvos 0.006â€“0.06 3.8 Ã— 10â€“3 Cabbage, fruit juice [81] CDs Molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensor Dimethoate 6.0 Ã— 10â€“4â€“3.4 Ã— 10â€“2 1.83 Ã— 10â€“5 Chinese cabbage, broccoli, cucumber [82] CDs-DNA aptamer Aptamer-molecularly imprinted sensor Lincomycin 5.0 Ã— 10â€“6â€“1.0 Ã— 10â€“3 1.6 Ã— 10â€“7 Meat [77] CDs-MoS2b Fluorescence determination Kanamycin 4â€“25 1.1 Milk [83] GQDs-PoAPc Voltammetric determination Levofloxacin 0.05â€“100 0.01 Milk [84] CDs-Fe3+ Fluorescence determination Oxytetracycline 0â€“3.5 2.28 Ã— 10â€“4 Milk [85] CDs Fluorescence determination Tetracycline 0.02â€“14 5.48 Ã— 10â€“3 Milk [86] CDs-MnO2 Photoelectrochemical immunoassay Aflatoxin B1 3.2 Ã— 10â€“5â€“6.4 Ã— 10â€“2 6.72 Ã— 10â€“6 Peanut [76] N-CDs Fluorescence determination Aflatoxin B1 1.6 Ã— 10â€“8â€“6.4 Ã— 10â€“6 1.6 Ã— 10â€“8 Peanut, corn [87] CDs- Chitosan Fluorescence determination Catechol 0.1â€“80 7.35 Ã— 10â€“2 â€” [88] CDs-AgNPs Fluorescence determination Melamine 0â€“2 2â€“20 0.03 Raw milk [89] CDs Fluorescence determination Melamine 0.2â€“6.0 2.85 Ã— 10â€“3 Milk [90] a: Not mentioned in the original text; b: Molybdenum disulfide; c: Poly-ortho-aminophenol. YE Song-Ling et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2017, 45(10): 1571â€“1581 References [1] Xu X Y, Ray R, Gu Y L, Ploehn H J, Gearheart L, Raker K, Scrivens W A. J. Am. Chem. Colloid Interface Sci., 2017, 501: 341â€“349 [5] Cheng C, Shi Y, Li M, Xing M, Wu Q. Mater. Sci. Eng., C, 2017, 79: 473â€“480 [6] Lv P, Yao Y, Li D, Zhou H, Naeem M A, Feng Q, Huang J, Cai Y, Wei Q. Carbohydr. Polym., 2017, 172: 93â€“101 [7] Zhou C, He X, Ya D, Zhong J, Deng B. Sens. Actuators, B, 2017, 249: 256â€“264 [8] Hou Y, Liu X, Tang X, Li T, Wu Q, Jiang Y, Yi J, Zhang G. item-info.ce:copyright: Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2016YFD0401204, 2016YFE0106000), the Guangdong Natural Science Funds for Distinguished Young Scholar of China (No. 2014A030306026), the Guangdong Special Support Program of China (No. 2014TQ01N109), and the Guangdong Provincial Science and Technology Projects of China (No. 2016A040403077).

head.ce:title: Preparation of Carbon Dots and Their Application in Food Analysis as Signal Probe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Safety and Quality, College of Food Science, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510640, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Safety and Quality

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Food and Drug Vocational College, Guangzhou 510520, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Food and Drug Vocational College

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a new class of fluorescent nanoparticles, carbon dots have potential to replace semiconductor quantum dots due to their excellent optical properties, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, facile synthesis, low cost and easy functionalization. By virtue of characteristics mentioned above, carbon dots-based fluorescent probes showed great potential in the field of food analysis. In this paper, we reviewed the production of carbon dots, their properties and applications in food analysis such as food additives detection, metal ion monitoring, food borne pathogens detection, organic pollutants and food nutritional components analysis. The challenges and perspectives of carbon dots in this exciting and promising field were also discussed.

Based on the unique fluorescence characteristics and high sensitivity, carbon dots can combine with other methods such as electrochemical sensor and molecularly imprinted polymer, etc., to develop more efficient and sensitive detection technologies for food analysis.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: J. Nat. Sci. Hunan Normal Univwesity
Spectrochim. Acta Part A, Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.
